issues.description: Session validation is completely skipped when Redis is unavailable, allowing authentication without valid session check. This creates a security bypass vulnerability when the Redis dependency goes down.
issues.suggestion: When Redis is unavailable, reject authentication requests instead of bypassing session validation. For high-availability requirements, implement a fallback mechanism like JWT-based session revocation list stored in a secondary cache or database. Never silently skip security controls under infrastructure failure.

issues.description: OTP verification uses direct string comparison ('storedCode !== code') instead of constant-time comparison, potentially vulnerable to timing attacks.
issues.suggestion: Use crypto.subtle.timingSafeEqual() or a constant-time comparison library to compare the stored OTP with the provided code. This prevents attackers from inferring the correct code via response time differences.

issues.description: No rate limiting on /auth/verify-email-code endpoint. An attacker could brute-force 6-digit OTPs (10,000 possibilities) with only 5 max attempts per 5 minutes per email - the current attempt limit is insufficient for a 6-digit code.
issues.suggestion: Add rate limit decorator to verify-email-code route similar to other auth routes. Consider increasing OTP_MAX_ATTEMPTS or using a longer/higher-entropy code format (e.g., alphanumeric 8+ characters). Implement exponential backoff after failed attempts.

issues.description: Rate limiting is completely bypassed in non-production environments (NODE_ENV !== 'production') when userId/email is not present. This allows attackers to circumvent rate limits during development/staging attacks.
issues.suggestion: Never bypass security controls based on environment. Rate limiting should apply in all environments. The dev-mode bypass should be removed or implemented differently (e.g., higher limits in dev rather than no limits).

issues.description: Password minimum length is only 6 characters, which is considered weak by modern security standards (OWASP recommends minimum 8 characters with complexity).
issues.suggestion: Increase minimum password length to at least 8 characters. Consider adding password complexity requirements (uppercase, lowercase, numbers, special characters) using @Matches decorator.

issues.description: No maximum length validation on password field in LoginDto. This could allow extremely long passwords that cause resource exhaustion during bcrypt hashing.
issues.suggestion: Add @MaxLength(128) decorator to the password field to prevent resource exhaustion attacks. Also validate max length on the server-side bcrypt hash operation.

issues.description: JWT token stored in localStorage is vulnerable to XSS attacks. If any XSS vulnerability exists in the application, attackers can steal tokens.
issues.suggestion: Store JWT tokens in httpOnly, secure cookies instead of localStorage. This prevents JavaScript access to the token, mitigating XSS-based token theft. Implement CSRF protection if using cookie-based auth.

issues.description: Security incident log message includes potentially attacker-controlled username field ('Source: ${payload.username ?? "unknown"}'), which could be used to inject malicious content into log aggregation systems.
issues.suggestion: Sanitize or truncate the username in log messages. Use a structured logging format that escaping special characters. Consider logging only a hash or partial identifier instead of raw username.

issues.description: The checkAuth() function in AuthContext makes an unauthenticated API call to /auth/profile on page load, which may leak information about valid user sessions to attackers.
issues.suggestion: Use a token validation endpoint that doesn't expose user data, or validate token client-side using the shared getTokenExpiryTime() before making authenticated requests. Only call /auth/profile if a valid-looking token exists.

issues.description: window.location.replace() is used for redirects without validation. An attacker could potentially manipulate the returnTo parameter in handleExpiredSession to redirect users to a malicious page.
issues.suggestion: Validate that redirect targets are within the application's trusted domains. Implement a whitelist of allowed redirect paths or use Next.js router methods with proper path validation.

issues.description: Session TTL is 1 hour (3600 seconds) which may be too short for some use cases, forcing frequent re-authentication. Additionally, there is no refresh token mechanism implemented.
issues.suggestion: Implement a JWT refresh token mechanism to allow extended sessions without compromising security. Use refresh tokens with httpOnly cookies and short-lived access tokens.

issues.description: Login DTO allows either username or email for authentication but LoginDto uses @IsOptional on username/email fields without @ValidateNested or a custom validator to ensure at least one is provided.
issues.suggestion: Add a custom decorator or use @ValidateAtLeastOneOf (if available) to ensure either username or email is provided, but not neither.
